commonIssues: Root rot can occur in wet, poorly-drained soils; ensure the container or bed drains freely (Missouri Botanical Garden).
Aphids may cluster on leaves and reduce vigor (RHS).
Leek rust (a fungal disease) shows as bright orange raised pustules on the foliage (RHS).
Avoid over-fertilizing; chives need only a very light feed (UMN Extension).
Shallow-rooted clump-former; an 8 L pot at least 20 cm deep suits container growing, larger is fine (RHS / Old Farmer's Almanac).

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension — Growing chives in home gardens: six to eight hours of direct light is best; tolerates light shade; water deeply so soil does not dry out around the root zone; pH 6.0-7.0; very light application of 5-10-5 fertilizer once each spring, container plants liquid-fed at half strength every four to six weeks; space plants 6 to 12 inches apart; plant after frost danger; flowers late May/June.
University of Minnesota Extension — Growing chives: feeding guidance (light 5-10-5 once each spring; container plants liquid-fed at half strength every four to six weeks).
University of Minnesota Extension — Growing chives: watering deeply to keep root zone from drying, with consistent moisture; drought tolerant but consistent watering needed for high yields.

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Allium schoenoprasum: USDA Zones 4 to 8; height/spread 1.0-1.5 ft; full sun to part shade; medium water; bloom April-May; no serious insect or disease problems, root rot may occur in wet, poorly-drained soils; easily grown in average, medium, well-drained soils.
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Allium schoenoprasum: medium water needs and root rot risk in wet, poorly-drained soils (overwater risk).
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Allium schoenoprasum: 'No serious insect or disease problems. Root rot may occur in wet, poorly-drained soils.'

Royal Horticultural Society — How to grow Chives: grow in sun or partial shade; containers at least 20 cm (8 in) wide and deep with peat-free soil-based compost; sow indoors early spring through summer, plant outdoors in spring; harvest late spring until die-back in autumn; space clumps at least 15 cm (6 in) apart; problems include aphids and leek rust.

Royal Horticultural Society — How to grow Chives: planting/sowing in spring, harvest from late spring until die-back in autumn (seasonality); established ground plants rarely need extra water except in drought, container plants need regular moisture.
Royal Horticultural Society — How to grow Chives: common problems are aphids (sap-sucking insects) and leek rust (fungal disease, bright orange raised pustules).

The Old Farmer's Almanac — Chives: shallow root system; if planting in a pot choose one roughly 8 to 12 inches deep; grow best in full sun, tolerate light shade, grow well in partial sun (4 to 6 hours per day); harvest 3-4 times the first year, cut back monthly thereafter.

Confidence note — heatStressC (30 C) is an estimate. Sources (Missouri Botanical Garden Zones 4-8, RHS, UMN) describe chives as a cold-hardy perennial that prefers cool conditions and may go semi-dormant or bolt in summer heat, but none give an explicit numeric heat-stress threshold; 30 C is a conservative onset-of-heat-stress value. coldHardyMinC (-34 C) is derived from the lower limit of USDA Zone 4 (the cold end of the Missouri Botanical Garden range) rather than a directly stated temperature.
